speaker: interviewer
text: November the 14th. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Fred Knoller, and we are in London, England. This is tape 2. You were saying how you tried to-- to get a-- a-- a path to get out of-- of Austria but couldn't. So you ended up there, but your brother Eric did go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Eric did go. And in the meantime, the November the 9th came, the Kristallnacht. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a terrible, terrible night that I remember. I don't know whether you know the story why, actually. There was a-- a-- a Polish Jew who killed a German diplomat in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German took this as an excuse and burned all the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] synagogues. In fact, I must tell you, I don't know whether you had-- I read the details of how the-- there was a historian who wrote in Vienna how many synagogues were burnt and how many people. Can I-- I have some notes here, [INAUDIBLE]? If you'd like to. If--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's just--

speaker: interviewee
text: --if it's historic, it will be documented.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- yeah. Well, because there in Vienna, there were 21 synagogues burnt-- synagogues burned down, 4,053 Jewish shops plundered and closed, 1,950 Jewish homes were ransacked, 7,800 Jews arrested. 680 Jews committed suicide, and 91 Jews were murdered. This was a night, again, when I-- which I will not forget. Because as I told you before, we lived on the third floor of an apartment building. Our windows were overlooking a courtyard, and we saw the flames. The-- there-- this-- the-- the-- the sky was red from flames.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a telephone call from a-- a very good friend of ours, [NON-ENGLISH] were the name, who lived on the other side of the courtyard overlooking [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was our synagogue there. And he phoned us up. He said, do you know-- to my parents-- do you know that our synagogue is burning completely? It's completely burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't sleep at all during the night, because [SIGHS] somewhere around in the region 11 or 12 o'clock at night we heard, in the courtyard, a noise of SS boots entering the courtyard. And we saw the voices of our housekeeper, [NON-ENGLISH]. We saw-- looking down from the window, we saw the [? SR, ?] people in brown uniform with their swastika armband. And they were talking to each other, and-- and they entered our entrance where we lived. You can imagine how frightened we were. They-- we heard a scream coming from the first floor. On the first floor was a Jewish couple who was in the fashion business. I think their name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- no, I'm sorry, that was another neighbor. No, it was Epstein, Epstein. And they had a big sign on the outside notifying that this is a-- a firm, Epstein and-- and Bruder, Epstein and Bruder-- Brothers, [GERMAN], you know, fashion. And that scream from the-- came from the first floor where they lived, and we were on the third floor. And suddenly, we saw-- we heard a smash of a window and the-- a dump, a bump. Somebody-- in fact, it was Mr. Epstein who hit the ground of the-- jumped from the first floor down to-- in the courtyard, and he was lying there. [SIGHS] Well, the [? SR ?] went off. And the next day, I encountered [NON-ENGLISH], our housekeeper. He said to me, look, they had to have a Jew. I told him there's no other Jew here in that-- in that building. So they left after they did Mr. Epstein. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the Kristallnacht, this was the time when everybody who experienced it knew that there's no way that you could live in a country like that. Because we knew all our friends-- a lot of people were arrested, were sent to camp, were-- and one of our friends was-- committed suicide. And people didn't have a business anymore. The Jews were-- couldn't continue any business. So Eric was already gone. My Uncle Bodek, with their three children, said, we are not going to live there. We are going to go illegally to Belgium. And so my parents said, why don't you take Freddy with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto was already in negotiations to leave as a medical student to-- to-- to England. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he, in fact, he did go afterwards, I knew, but he went via Holland, and the Dutch people didn't want to let him in. He was sent back, and then he went to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my-- I [? expect ?] my parents, look, the Bodeks go. Why don't you come? Let's go all together. Let's go to Belgium. Let's-- said, no, nothing will happen to us. We are not going to go. We have that apartment here, you know. I'm still working. My father was still working as an accountant. And-- and no, we're not. Maybe later on, but not-- you go. So I went with my cousins and my uncle and aunt to Belgium-- How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --illegally.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Aix-la-Chapelle, which-- Aachen-- which is on the border between Belgium and Germany. We went by train, first Cologne. In Cologne, we met a guide who we had to pay, and he would take us across the border [? with ?] one of his friends. And we had to pay quite a bit of money. We went to Aachen at night, and there we were assembled in a barn, you know, a farm. And he told us, you must be very, very quiet, and we'll walk. I will show you where to walk. And nobody, but nobody, was allowed to make a move or cry or-- or-- or say anything. We were approximately-- we were approximately 20 people, 20 Jews. And he took us, at night, through a forest, and it was quite traumatic, actually, the fear of being discovered and-- and-- but he pointed out to us that the-- the main thing is that you avoid the Belgium, because if they catch you they may well return you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told us exactly where to go, and we went to a village, forgotten the name, where they put us into a private car, you know, several private cars. Took us to the biggest town nearby, and we took the train and went to Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must say, this Fraulein Schiff, that was our neighbor, gave me the address of her-- another cousin who lives in Antwerp by the name of Joss Apte, A-P-T-E, who was a diamond merchant in Antwerp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She-- I arrived in Antwerp somewhere around in the region of 7:00 in the morning. And I had the address, and I went to that-- I always forget-- I'll always remember [NON-ENGLISH]-- in-- in [FLEMISH], in Flemish it is [FLEMISH]-- and beautiful building, you know, apartment house. And it's 7:00, and I was hungry and cold. And said, it's too early, really, to ring the bell. But at 7:30, I couldn't stand it anymore, and-- and I rang the bell, and they opened up. And I told him about me, and they received me, gave me cup of coffee or something. And then he came, Mr. Apte, came with me to the Jewish community in Antwerp, introduced me. And in Belgium, the Jewish community took care of the refugees. We got pocket money. We got food, you know, free meals in the community center. We got-- they arranged for me to-- with another refugee-- to go and take a-- live in a-- in a room-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] breakfast and, you know, like B and B, bed and breakfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your cousins and aunt and uncle now too?

words.text: and aunt and uncle now too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousins went to Brussels. They felt-- they had some contacts in Brussels and address. Now, I want to say that my cousins-- he was a tailor. The youngest, Max, was a tailor, and Leo, he was a furrier. He made furs. So they had very good professions. And through their contacts in their trade, they knew people in Brussels. And they, again, the Jewish community, they took care in Brussels, took care of them. And we remained in contact. I was in Antwerp, and he was in Brussels. I sometimes went to Brussels. And-- and we still kept very much in contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were doing quite well. They earned their money. I didn't. I wasn't able to do any-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] any work, because I only just started tailoring. But when I spoke with-- told the Jewish community that I'm a cellist, they said, well, do you have a cello? I said, no, I haven't got. [LAUGHS] I had to leave it in Vienna. I couldn't go across the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, OK, we'll get you one, and they introduced me to a-- an orchestra. And I joined the orchestra and got paid little bit whenever they had concerts. I was the-- the last cellist in the-- in the row. As I played the cello, I also played a little bit the bass, the big bass. And I love jazz. I always loved music, and-- and somehow or other, I played in nightclubs sometimes. And whenever-- if they had a bass, I was able to borrow it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt fairly safe at this-- at this period of time in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I felt extremely safe. I stayed in contact with my parents. We wrote to each other very, very often. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, maybe I'm jumping the gun a little bit, but my brother just discovered recently that he kept hundreds of letters that my parents wrote from Vienna to him in Florida.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they mention that-- why don't you write, they ask my brother Eric? Why don't you write? Freddy writes from Belgium very often. So just having read these just now recently, I must have been in contact all the time with my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- You were playing in the clubs and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Playing in the clubs. And I was-- I had a little room there with-- with a friend of mine. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the life was-- it was OK. We naturally knew what is happening. We-- we read the papers and read what's happening in Vienna. And--and we always were very keen to hear, you know. And I spoke to my parents sometimes by telephone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] organization, who kept me, to a certain extent, said, look here, we have now-- we have a camp for young people, for Jewish refugees. And I think it would be much better if you would go there and meet all the-- all your friends there. We-- we-- somehow, I think they were pressured by the Belgian government to keep us all together. We went to-- to a place called Merxplas, and it was a camp for Jewish refugees from Austria, from Germany. And we organized-- we had our own organization there. We were not guarded in any way, but they made us help the-- the peasants in the-- in the fields, you know, gave us some work. We-- we had a theatre group and [? free ?] entertainment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there from 1940-- may I? Just I-- I made some notes of it, actually. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I went-- Merxplas-- I think 1940 I went-- 1940. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, it was prior to 1940, because the Germans invaded Belgium in May 19-- May the 10th, 1940. So from Merxplas, I then was asked by the-- by the Jewish organization, look, we are forming an orchestra in-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a young group, a lot of lovely people. Why don't you go there? So they said, we'll give you-- we'll lend you the cello, and you play with your orchestra. [NON-ENGLISH] was another--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], again, another Jewish camp, but it was more for younger people, whereas in the Merxplas we had of every age. But they concentrated the younger people in this camp. And it was actually a lovely camp, terrific orchestra. There were a lot of musicians there. And-- and we really-- as far as I remember, it was a very pleasant place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10th of--

speaker: interviewer
text: May?

speaker: interviewee
text: May, the war started. The-- we heard the German planes coming. And we were told, come on. You want to stay here? You stay. You can do now-- the-- the-- the leaders of the camp said, look here, I think we all should try to run away and go. Some could go to France. Some-- maybe if you go to the coast, maybe you can get a ship, go to England, et cetera. And I went with a group of boys to-- walked from-- from [NON-ENGLISH] to-- towards France. We were able to-- we arrived at a bigger town. I wish I remembered the name of the town. And we were at the station, trying to take a train at the station, when the German planes came, and they bombed this particular station. This was terrible. We were really in the middle of it. I-- I was just-- they were building a-- some building material, and I was hiding underneath some big tubes. And-- and the bombs were really falling all around, and we lost quite a few people in that-- in that raid, quite a few boys. We went-- we walked from there to-- towards the border. We crossed the border and arrived in Lille. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Lille, we were arrested by the French police, asked for identification papers. And I showed them my passport, my German passport, which had on it a big red J on it. And I told him, look, we are Jews, refugees from Nazis, and we're coming to run away from the Germans. He said, no, you are German. You are enemy alien. And they arrested us and taking us to-- by train for-- it took us nearly two days to arrive to a camp, which was called Saint-Cyprien. Saint-Cyprien was right on the Spanish border, near the Spanish border. It was a-- the camp-- if you remember before, in 1936 I think, you had a civil war in Spain. And a lot of the Spanish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] patriots, I mean, the-- the-- the left-wing Spanish, when Spain was occupied by-- or won-- when Franco won the war, they left as refugees. And they were put by France-- they went over the Pyrenees, went-- were put into this particular camp. It was approximately 10 kilometers from a town called Perpignan. And we were-- when we were arrested and transported, we were in a train guarded by French soldiers. But not only Jews, but there were real Germans, fifth columnist, which they arrested, and they put us with Nazi Germans into this particular camp. We, naturally, Jews, had continuous fight with the fifth columnists, with the Nazi Germans. And they were very antisemitic and called us names-- [GERMAN], et cetera, and we wouldn't let them do it. And we called the camp commander and-- and said, look here, you are putting us together in barracks together with these Nazis. And you know, you must realize that we are anti-Nazis, that we are refugees from the Nazis. We cannot possibly go and stay in a barracks where we have continuous fights. So he realized. He got the Jews out, and we got our own barracks. But on the-- [SIGHS] this camp, I must explain, this camp was bordering onto the sea. It was-- we had-- there was a beach, but it was all wired up. That means we couldn't go through into the water. It was all wired up. And underneath the wires, you know, we saw there was a big concrete-- very large concrete area. And we were told that this was an area where a mass grave for the Spanish refugees when they caught-- there was a typhus [FRENCH] here. And hundreds and hundreds of these refugees died there, and they were put into this grave, and they put this concrete block over it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a friend there in the hospital, a Jewish doctor. And one day he said to me, you know, we are getting so much of the illnesses here, and we believe it's typhus. We didn't get very much food there, and the-- we had-- we had started to get lice on our-- on our clothes and on our-- so I remembered a little bit that typhus come-- could come from lice. [SIGHS] I said, oh, this is not for me. I must get out of here. We were guarded by Sudanese soldiers from Sudan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew then that my cousins from-- oh, one was in the camp, yeah, it was in-- in Saint-Cyprien with me. That was Rosie's future husband, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he told me that the Bodeks are living in Limoges, which was in-- in France, central France-- it was-- which I knew later on it was in the non-occupied zone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I-- at nighttime I said, I must leave there. It wasn't so difficult, you know. You didn't have to-- the Sudanese soldiers who guarded us-- we had wire. I cut-- I went through the wire and-- and left the-- left the camp. It wasn't terribly difficult for a young fellow like me. And I knew that the nearest town was Perpignan. So I said, I'm going to go direction of Perpignan. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I walked all night and arrived in the morning in Perpignan, rather outside Perpignan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just as it became light, I said to myself, oh, I must go and just have a little bit of sleep. So I was in the forest, and I slept an hour or two. Then I went into town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I didn't really know what I would do. Now what? How-- if I tell the French I'm here, they arrest me again. So on the square, I saw a group of Boy Scouts. And I remember I used to be a Boy Scout in Vienna. So I went to one of them, and I gave him the greeting, Boy Scout greeting, like that. Oh, he returned it. So this one fellow who looked sympathetic, and I told him, look, I'm a Jew. I run away from the Nazis. I'm a-- a refugee from the Nazis. And somehow or other, I have to go and get to my cousins in Limoges. And my French was very bad at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me-- took to his home, introduced me to his parents. And the parents gave me coffee and breakfast and-- and treated me wonderful, gave me some money, gave me a ticket to go-- train ticket-- to go to Limoges. And it was really tremendous. I wish I remember the name of the people, but slip my mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Limoges, I went to the address that I knew that my cousins lived there, that they-- they-- house to themselves with a garden, and-- and-- and they were very happy to receive me. I slept in the same room with [? Maxwell, ?] with the youngest. And we-- ah, by that time, naturally, France was occupied by the Germans. Because Belgium was occupied on the 10th. France was occupied by the 14th of-- of May. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] however, if you remember, the French occupied the northern part, and Limoges was in the unoccupied zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we felt quite safe, but I couldn't really do anything. There was a Jewish organization there, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- there was a-- we ate in the-- in the community center. And again, we were given some pocket money. But I wanted to play music. So I was unable to get hold of a cello, because I left the cello in [NON-ENGLISH]. I couldn't carry it with me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I said to my cousin-- to my uncle, said, look, I want to go and retrieve my cello that I had in [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, you must be mad. You go in that-- Into the occupied zone? I said, look, I can earn my living here. I'll come back. I had a bit of money saved up, and they gave me some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took the train, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] went to the border of the occupied zone. I went illegally across there into the occupied zone, went to the next town. Took the train, went to-- to Belgium, you know, that-- to find the-- the cello and maybe clothes, because I hardly-- I didn't have any clothes whatsoever because everything was left there. I found a sympathetic Belgium who took me to [NON-ENGLISH] by car. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I looked around. The place was completely destroyed. Every-- everything was ransacked. I couldn't find anything. There was no cello, no clothes, no-- even barracks were destroyed. I don't know whether the Germans entered there, had a camp, or something like that. But I couldn't find anything, none whatsoever. Said, OK. He took me to the station again. I took the train to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like you just did these things. You didn't think too much about all of the risks involved in them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must tell you something. Now, for instance, if I think about it, it said how stupid one can be. I'm in the unoccupied zone. I go into the lion's den. I was, as a young fellow, without fear whatsoever. I was completely-- I was naive and young and without fear. I didn't think. I wanted a cello. Like, when I was in-- picking up the-- the-- the-- the-- the train, you know, to go to Paris, and then on the train I said to me, you're going to Paris and change the train to go to Limoges. I must be-- you must be mad. I mean, you have never seen Paris-- the Eiffel Tower, Arc de Triomphe, the Champs-Elysees, Place Pigalle, the night life of Paris. You must go and see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can I as a-- how could I have done this, you know, without any fear whatsoever, without even thinking about it? I mean, it's so stupid now. Nowadays, when I-- how stupid can a young person be? And I must say, I was very young. And this is actually how I arrived in Paris.
